evals.prompt: I have 500 bank transactions and 510 ledger entries. Most are exact matches, but 20 have slightly different names like 'AWS' vs 'Amazon'. How can I reconcile this?
evals.expected_output: Should recommend a two-step process: Deterministic matching for exact entries and Probabilistic/Fuzzy matching for name variances. Should mention vendor name normalization. Should provide a Reconciliation Report structure.
assertions: Suggests deterministic matching for exact matches
Recommends fuzzy matching for name variances
Mentions vendor name normalization
Identifies many-to-one or one-to-many issues
Output follows Reconciliation Report structure

evals.prompt: Our bank rec auto-matches 92% of items but the 8% manual queue has grown to 1,400 items. Diagnose and fix.
evals.expected_output: Analyze unmatched population by pattern: missing alias rules, batched settlements needing one-to-many matching, FX/fee tolerance too tight, stale items needing write-off policy. Quantify each cluster before adding rules.
assertions: Clusters the unmatched queue by root cause pattern before proposing fixes
Suggests one-to-many/subset-sum matching for batched settlements
Reviews tolerance and alias rules with false-positive guardrails
Addresses the aged backlog with an escalation/write-off policy

evals.prompt: Finance wants to auto-clear all reconciling differences under 5,000 to P&L. Sensible?
evals.expected_output: Conditionally - small-difference auto-clear is standard, but require: a single materiality-based threshold approved by the controller, logging of every auto-cleared item, monthly trend monitoring (many small differences can hide systematic error or fraud), and direction analysis (consistent one-way differences are a red flag).
assertions: Accepts the practice in principle with governance conditions
Requires logging and review of auto-cleared items
Flags one-directional or trending differences as systematic risk
Sets the threshold by documented materiality, not convenience

evals.prompt: Reconcile the attached May bank statement (evals/files/bank_statement.csv) against the GL cash ledger (evals/files/gl_cash.csv). Both start from an agreed opening balance of $100,000.00. Match transactions, identify deposits in transit, outstanding cheques, and bank-only items, then produce a bank reconciliation statement proving the adjusted balances agree.
evals.expected_output: Bank ending balance $147,060.00; GL ending balance $154,540.00. Deposit in transit $27,300.00 (INV1045); outstanding cheques $12,640.00 (2206) and $7,120.00 (2207); bank-only items: charges $350.00 and interest $410.00 to be booked in GL. Adjusted balance both sides $154,600.00.
assertions: Computes bank ending balance as $147,060.00
Computes GL ending balance as $154,540.00
Identifies the $27,300.00 INV1045 receipt as a deposit in transit
Identifies cheques 2206 ($12,640.00) and 2207 ($7,120.00) as outstanding
Identifies bank charges $350.00 and interest $410.00 as unbooked GL items
Proves adjusted balances agree at $154,600.00
Performs the matching with code

evals.prompt: [adversarial] Reconcile the attached June files (evals/files/bank_dirty.csv vs evals/files/gl_june.csv), opening balances agreed at $50,000.
evals.expected_output: Must flag B3 as a duplicate bank posting of cheque 2210 (potential bank error - only one GL entry exists) and B4 as a malformed non-numeric amount needing source correction; must NOT guess B4's value silently or treat the duplicate as an outstanding item.
assertions: Flags the duplicated cheque 2210 (refs B2/B3) as a likely bank error, not an outstanding item
Flags the non-numeric 'TWENTY THOUSAND' amount as unprocessable without correction
Does not silently impute a value for the malformed row when computing totals
Completes the reconciliation for clean items and lists the two exceptions
Processes with code
